channel.title: CTF on M15tak3のBlog
channel.copyright: All articles on this blog are licensed under the BY-NC-SA license agreement unless otherwise stated. Please indicate the source when reprinting!

item.title: CTF Traffic Capture Analysis
item.author: skyman.soul@gmail.com (M15tak3)
item.description:
CTF Traffic Capture Analysis

Author: M15tak3(skyman.soul@gmail.com)

CTF Traffic Capture: Extracting the Flag from PCAP

In CTF traffic challenges, you are usually given a .pcap, .pcapng, or a compressed archive, and asked to recover a flag from network communication records.

These problems are usually classified under Misc, Forensics, or Traffic Analysis. The core skill is not memorizing commands, but reconstructing what happened on the network.

In short, traffic challenges ask:

Who talked to whom? Which protocol was used? What data was sent? Was it encoded, compressed, split, or hidden?

As long as you follow this logic step by step, you can solve most beginner-to-intermediate traffic tasks.

1. Common Tools

1.1 Wireshark

Wireshark is the most common GUI tool for traffic challenges.

It lets you inspect each packet and filter by protocol, IP, port, or keywords.

Common features:

  • Protocol statistics
  • IP conversations
  • Filters for HTTP, DNS, FTP, ICMP, etc.
  • Follow TCP stream
  • Export HTTP transferred files
  • Inspect plaintext in packets

After opening a capture, two key entries are:

Statistics -> Protocol Hierarchy
Statistics -> Conversations

The first shows which protocols exist; the second shows which hosts communicated.

1.2 tshark

tshark is the CLI version of Wireshark, great for batch extraction.

Show basic capture content:

tshark -r traffic.pcap

Show HTTP requests:

tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y "http.request"

Extract DNS query names:

tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y "dns.qry.name" -T fields -e dns.qry.name

Extract ICMP data field:

tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y "icmp" -T fields -e data

1.3 strings

strings is useful for quick plaintext keyword scanning.

strings traffic.pcap | grep -i flag
strings traffic.pcap | grep -i password
strings traffic.pcap | grep -i admin
strings traffic.pcap | grep -i upload

For easy tasks, strings may directly find the flag. If not, it can still reveal suspicious requests, credentials, or filenames.

1.4 binwalk and foremost

If file content was transferred in the capture (images, archives, docs), try file carving:

binwalk traffic.pcap

foremost traffic.pcap

These are supporting tools. In traffic analysis, protocol and conversation analysis usually come first.

2. Basic Workflow After Getting a Capture

2.1 Read the challenge description first

The description usually gives direction, for example:

  • Password leaked during admin login
  • Hacker uploaded a file
  • Secret hidden in DNS queries
  • Flag sent through ICMP
  • Recover keyboard input
  • Find what attacker downloaded

If it mentions HTTP, prioritize HTTP requests and responses. If it mentions DNS, prioritize domain extraction. If it mentions keyboard, consider USB HID traffic. If it mentions upload/download, pay attention to exported files.

Often the description already tells you the key protocol.

2.2 Check protocol statistics

In Wireshark:

Statistics -> Protocol Hierarchy

Focus on:

  • Plaintext protocols such as HTTP, FTP, DNS, ICMP
  • Special traffic such as USB, Bluetooth, 802.11
  • TCP/UDP ratio anomalies
  • Presence of encrypted TLS traffic

Examples:

  • Heavy HTTP proportion: likely web requests/responses matter
  • Many strange long DNS names: possible DNS tunneling
  • Many ICMP packets: inspect data
  • FTP present: look for plaintext credentials and transferred files

2.3 Check conversations

Open:

Statistics -> Conversations

Focus on:

  • Which IP pair has the most traffic
  • External suspicious IPs
  • Large data-volume connections
  • Suspicious ports
  • Obvious upload/download behavior

Common ports to remember:

21    FTP
22    SSH
25    SMTP
53    DNS
80    HTTP
110   POP3
143   IMAP
443   HTTPS
3306  MySQL
6379  Redis

If one TCP conversation is much larger than others, it may contain file transfer or critical data.

3. Common Wireshark Filters

3.1 Filter by protocol 

http
dns
ftp
icmp
tcp
udp
usb

3.2 Filter by IP 

ip.addr == 192.168.1.10
ip.src == 192.168.1.10
ip.dst == 192.168.1.10

3.3 Filter by port 

tcp.port == 80
tcp.srcport == 8080
tcp.dstport == 21
udp.port == 53

3.4 Search by keyword 

frame contains "flag"
frame contains "password"
frame contains "admin"
frame contains "ctf"

For HTTP:

http contains "flag"
http contains "password"

3.5 Inspect one TCP stream 

tcp.stream eq 0
tcp.stream eq 1
tcp.stream eq 2

Right-click a TCP packet:

Follow -> TCP Stream

This is one of the most important operations in traffic tasks. A single packet might be incomplete, but stream reassembly shows full request/response content.

4. HTTP Traffic Analysis

HTTP is very common in CTF traffic because it is often plaintext.

4.1 Filter HTTP requests

Only HTTP:

http

Only HTTP requests:

http.request

Only GET requests:

http.request.method == "GET"

Only POST requests:

http.request.method == "POST"

POST is especially important for logins, form submissions, and uploads.

4.2 What to inspect in HTTP

Common clue locations:

  • URL parameters
  • POST body
  • Cookie
  • Authorization header
  • Referer
  • User-Agent
  • Response body
  • Uploaded file content
  • Returned archives/images/scripts

Example:

POST /login.php HTTP/1.1
Host: example.com
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

username=admin&password=YWRtaW4xMjM=

YWRtaW4xMjM= looks like Base64. Decoded value:

admin123

This credential may be the key for archive extraction, panel login, or flag submission.

4.3 Export HTTP objects

If files were transferred via HTTP:

File -> Export Objects -> HTTP

Then check file type:

file suspicious_file

If archive: try extract. If image: consider steganography. If script: inspect source code. If filename/response/comment contains strange strings: attempt decoding.

5. FTP Traffic Analysis

FTP is often friendly for CTF because usernames, passwords, and commands may be plaintext.

Filter FTP:

ftp

Common content:

USER admin
PASS 123456

If file transfer exists, inspect:

ftp-data

You can follow related TCP streams to reconstruct transmitted content.

Typical FTP task goals:

  • Find credentials
  • Find uploaded/downloaded filenames
  • Restore transferred files
  • Continue decryption or hidden-content analysis on extracted files

6. DNS Tunneling Analysis

DNS challenges often split data into subdomains.

Filter DNS:

dns

Extract query names:

tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y "dns.qry.name" -T fields -e dns.qry.name

You may see:

ZmxhZ3s.example.com
ZG5zXw.example.com
c2VjcmV0fQ.example.com

Take and concatenate the left parts:

ZmxhZ3sZG5zXwc2VjcmV0fQ

Then try Base64, Hex, URL decode, etc.

Common DNS tunneling signs:

  • Very long subdomains
  • Random-looking domain labels
  • Fragments that can be concatenated across many requests
  • Clear Base64-like character sets
  • Abnormally high query frequency

If the domain contains -, _, digits, mixed case, consider Base64 URL-safe variants.

7. ICMP Traffic Analysis

ICMP is the protocol used by ping. In CTF, data is often hidden in ICMP data fields.

Filter ICMP:

icmp

Extract ICMP data:

tshark -r traffic.pcap -Y "icmp" -T fields -e data

If you get hex like:

666c61677b69636d705f7365637265747d

Decode with Python:

data = "666c61677b69636d705f7365637265747d"
print(bytes.fromhex(data).decode())

Output:

flag{icmp_secret}

Remember deduplication. Ping usually has request and reply; concatenating all ICMP payloads may duplicate data.

Only requests:

icmp.type == 8

Only replies:

icmp.type == 0

8. TCP Stream Following

TCP stream following is a core operation in traffic challenges.

In Wireshark, select a TCP packet and right-click:

Follow -> TCP Stream

This shows full client-server communication for that connection.

Switch streams with:

tcp.stream eq 0
tcp.stream eq 1
tcp.stream eq 2

Prioritize stream-following when:

  • HTTP content appears incomplete
  • FTP transfers files
  • Suspicious port has large data
  • You only see a partial flag or encoded fragment
  • Data is split into many packets

After opening a stream, try display formats:

ASCII
Raw
Hex Dump

If needed, choose Raw and save original data.

9. USB Keyboard Traffic Analysis

Some challenges are not network captures but USB captures. A common task is reconstructing keyboard input.

Filter USB data:

usb.capdata

Extract field:

tshark -r usb.pcap -T fields -e usb.capdata

Keyboard HID data often looks like:

00:00:04:00:00:00:00:00
00:00:05:00:00:00:00:00
00:00:06:00:00:00:00:00

The third byte usually indicates key code:

04 -> a
05 -> b
06 -> c

If first byte is 02 or 20, Shift is typically pressed, so convert to uppercase/symbols.

Typical USB keyboard workflow:

  1. Extract usb.capdata
  2. Remove empty entries
  3. Take key codes
  4. Map using HID keyboard table
  5. Handle special keys: Shift, Backspace, Enter

10. Common Encodings and Decoding Ideas

Flags in captures are not always directly visible. They are often encoded.

10.1 Base64

Features:

  • Usually letters, digits, +, /
  • May end with =
  • Length often multiple of 4

Example:

ZmxhZ3t0ZXN0X2ZsYWd9

Decode:

echo ZmxhZ3t0ZXN0X2ZsYWd9 | base64 -d

10.2 Hex

Features:

  • Only 0-9a-fA-F
  • Length usually even

Example:

666c61677b6865785f656e636f64657d

Python decode:

print(bytes.fromhex("666c61677b6865785f656e636f64657d").decode())

10.3 URL encoding

Example:

%66%6c%61%67%7b%75%72%6c%7d

Python decode:

from urllib.parse import unquote

print(unquote("%66%6c%61%67%7b%75%72%6c%7d"))

10.4 Compressed data signatures

If you see these magic bytes, consider decompression or file recovery:

1f 8b       gzip
78 9c       zlib
50 4b       zip
89 50 4e 47 png
ff d8 ff    jpg

Be sensitive to magic bytes. Sometimes transferred data is a real file, even if extension is fake.

11. A General Troubleshooting Checklist

After receiving a capture, follow this order:

  1. Read challenge description and infer likely protocols.
  2. Use strings to search flag, admin, password, ctf.
  3. In Wireshark, inspect Protocol Hierarchy.
  4. Use Conversations to find high-volume sessions.
  5. Filter common protocols: HTTP, FTP, DNS, ICMP.
  6. For suspicious TCP connections, use Follow TCP Stream.
  7. If HTTP transferred files, use Export Objects.
  8. Try Base64/Hex/URL decode on suspicious strings.
  9. Continue with stego/decompression/script analysis on exported files.
  10. If data is fragmented, extract fields with tshark and merge via script.

Not every challenge requires every step, but this flow prevents blind random clicking.

12. Practical Experience Tips

12.1 Do not only search for flag

Many tasks do not directly expose flag{}. You may first find a password, archive, image, script, or key.

Besides flag, also search:

admin
password
pass
login
upload
download
secret
key
token
ctf

12.2 Prioritize large-data connections

If one TCP connection has significantly more data than others, it often carries key content or files.

12.3 Prioritize plaintext protocols

HTTP, FTP, DNS, ICMP are often the fastest path in CTF traffic problems. If these protocols exist, analyze them first before heavy TLS-focused work.

12.4 Decode abnormal long strings immediately

Unusually long strings in URL, Cookie, POST params, DNS subdomains, or ICMP data are often intentional clues.

12.5 Script repetitive work

If you need to combine hundreds of DNS queries, ICMP payloads, or USB key events, do not do it manually. Extract with tshark, process with Python, and reduce mistakes.

13. Summary

Typical traffic-analysis route in CTF:

Description -> Protocol Statistics -> Conversation Analysis -> Protocol Filtering -> TCP Stream Following -> Data Extraction -> Decoding -> File Analysis

For HTTP: inspect requests, responses, cookies, POST body, and exported files. For FTP: look for credentials and transferred files. For DNS: extract domain labels and attempt concatenation/decoding. For ICMP: inspect data fields. For USB: reconstruct input with HID keyboard mapping.

Published on 2026-05-15 at M15tak3のBlog, last modified on 2026-05-15

All articles on this blog are licensed under the BY-NC-SA license agreement unless otherwise stated. Please indicate the source when reprinting!

item.title: CTF CSV Challenge Tutorial
item.author: skyman.soul@gmail.com (M15tak3)
item.description:
CTF CSV Challenge Tutorial

Author: M15tak3(skyman.soul@gmail.com)

CTF CSV Challenge Tutorial: What the Challenge Wants and How to Write the Python Script

CSV challenges in CTFs are usually placed under Misc, data analysis, forensics, or scripting. They look like ordinary spreadsheet files, but the real test is not whether you can use Excel. The real test is whether you can find patterns in a large amount of structured data, extract the useful information, and finally recover the flag.

In one sentence: a CSV challenge wants you to process data with a script instead of reading it line by line with your eyes.

1. What a CSV Challenge Usually Wants You to Do

A CSV file is essentially a text file whose fields are separated by commas, for example:

id,char,index
1,f,0
2,l,1
3,a,2
4,g,3

In CTF challenges, the flag may be split, shuffled, hidden, or encoded inside the table. Common goals include the following.

1.1 Search for the Flag Directly

The simplest case is that the flag is hidden in a certain row, column, or note field. The challenge may give you a very large CSV file so that it is hard to find manually.

What you need to do:

  1. Read the CSV.
  2. Iterate through every row.
  3. Search every field for keywords such as flag{, ctf{, or FLAG{.

1.2 Sort by One Column and Concatenate Characters

Many challenges split the flag into individual characters and then shuffle their order, for example:

pos,ch
3,g
0,f
2,a
1,l

Here, the challenge wants you to sort by pos and then concatenate ch:

flag

The key is to identify the “order column” and the “character column”.

1.3 Filter Valid Rows by Condition

Some CSV files contain a lot of noise, and only rows matching a condition are real. For example:

id,value,valid
1,x,0
2,f,1
3,l,1
4,?,0
5,a,1
6,g,1

The challenge wants you to keep rows where valid=1 and then concatenate value.

The core idea is: filter first, extract next, then concatenate.

1.4 Decode Encoded Fields

The CSV may not store characters directly. It may store ASCII, hex, binary, Base64, or other encodings.

For example:

index,ascii
0,102
1,108
2,97
3,103

Here, 102 108 97 103 corresponds to ASCII characters:

flag

So you need to convert numbers into characters:

chr(102)  # f

1.5 Reconstruct an Image or QR Code from Coordinates

A slightly more advanced CSV challenge may give you coordinates and colors, for example:

x,y,color
0,0,0
1,0,255
0,1,255
1,1,0

This kind of challenge may want you to reconstruct an image, a QR code, or a pixel map from the CSV. You need to:

  1. Read the x and y coordinates.
  2. Draw pixels according to the color values.
  3. Save the result as an image.
  4. Scan the QR code or inspect the image to get the flag.

2. What to Check First After Getting a CSV File

Do not start by writing a complicated script. Do some basic observation first.

2.1 Check the Header

The header often reveals the intended solving path, for example:

index, char, order, ascii, hex, x, y, r, g, b, valid

Different fields usually point to different directions:

Field name Possible meaning
index / pos / order Sorting
char / ch / value Character concatenation
ascii ASCII to character
hex Hex decoding
bin Binary decoding
x / y Coordinate drawing
r / g / b RGB image
valid / flag / is_true Filtering valid data

2.2 Check the Amount of Data

If there are only dozens of rows, you can inspect the pattern manually. If there are thousands or tens of thousands of rows, you must write a script.

import csv

with open("data.csv", "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    rows = list(reader)

print("Row count:", len(rows))
print("Header:", reader.fieldnames)
print("First 5 rows:")
for row in rows[:5]:
    print(row)

2.3 Check for Abnormal Values

CTF challenges often hide key information in abnormal values, such as:

  • A column has one unusually long row.
  • A row contains { or }.
  • Some numbers clearly fall in the ASCII range: 32 to 126.
  • Some fields look like hex: 66 6c 61 67.
  • Some fields look like Base64, possibly ending with =.

3. Basic Python CSV Reading

Prefer Python’s standard csv library first. It does not require installing third-party packages.

3.1 Read as Dictionaries

If the CSV has a header, DictReader is the most convenient option.

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        print(row)

Suppose the CSV is:

index,char
0,f
1,l

Each row will be read as:

{"index": "0", "char": "f"}

Note: values read from a CSV are strings by default. If you need sorting or calculation, convert them to int manually.

4. Script Template 1: Search for the Flag Directly

Use this when the challenge gives you a huge CSV and you suspect the flag is directly hidden inside it.

import csv
import re

filename = "data.csv"
pattern = re.compile(r"flag\{.*?\}", re.IGNORECASE)

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.reader(f)

    for line_no, row in enumerate(reader, start=1):
        for col_no, cell in enumerate(row, start=1):
            match = pattern.search(cell)
            if match:
                print(f"[+] Found flag at row {line_no}, column {col_no}:")
                print(match.group())

If the contest uses a flag format other than flag{}, change this line:

pattern = re.compile(r"your_prefix\{.*?\}", re.IGNORECASE)

5. Script Template 2: Sort and Concatenate Characters

Use this when the CSV has columns such as index and char.

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

items = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        index = int(row["index"])
        char = row["char"]
        items.append((index, char))

items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

flag = "".join(char for index, char in items)

print(flag)

The most common thing to change is the column name. If the challenge uses pos and value, change it to:

index = int(row["pos"])
char = row["value"]

6. Script Template 3: Filter Valid Rows and Concatenate

Use this when the CSV has a column marking whether a row is valid, such as valid, is_flag, or status.

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

result = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        if row["valid"] == "1":
            result.append(row["value"])

flag = "".join(result)
print(flag)

If sorting is also required, combine filtering and sorting:

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

items = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        if row["valid"] == "1":
            items.append((int(row["index"]), row["value"]))

items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

flag = "".join(value for index, value in items)
print(flag)

7. Script Template 4: ASCII to Characters

Use this when a field contains numbers such as 102,108,97,103.

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

chars = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        num = int(row["ascii"])
        chars.append(chr(num))

flag = "".join(chars)
print(flag)

If the CSV also contains an order column:

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

items = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        index = int(row["index"])
        char = chr(int(row["ascii"]))
        items.append((index, char))

items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

flag = "".join(char for index, char in items)
print(flag)

8. Script Template 5: Hex Decoding

The CSV may contain hex values such as 66, 6c, 61, and 67.

import csv

filename = "data.csv"

hex_parts = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        hex_parts.append(row["hex"])

hex_string = "".join(hex_parts)
result = bytes.fromhex(hex_string).decode()

print(result)

If each row contains a complete hex string, you can decode it row by row:

text = bytes.fromhex(row["hex"]).decode()

9. Script Template 6: Reconstruct an Image from Coordinates

If the CSV contains x, y, value, or r, g, b, it probably wants you to draw an image.

Install Pillow first:

pip install pillow

Black-and-white image reconstruction:

import csv
from PIL import Image

filename = "data.csv"

points = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        x = int(row["x"])
        y = int(row["y"])
        value = int(row["value"])
        points.append((x, y, value))

width = max(x for x, y, value in points) + 1
height = max(y for x, y, value in points) + 1

img = Image.new("L", (width, height), 255)

for x, y, value in points:
    color = 0 if value == 1 else 255
    img.putpixel((x, y), color)

img.save("result.png")
print("[+] Saved result.png")

RGB image reconstruction:

import csv
from PIL import Image

filename = "data.csv"

points = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        x = int(row["x"])
        y = int(row["y"])
        r = int(row["r"])
        g = int(row["g"])
        b = int(row["b"])
        points.append((x, y, r, g, b))

width = max(x for x, y, r, g, b in points) + 1
height = max(y for x, y, r, g, b in points) + 1

img = Image.new("RGB", (width, height), (255, 255, 255))

for x, y, r, g, b in points:
    img.putpixel((x, y), (r, g, b))

img.save("result.png")
print("[+] Saved result.png")

After saving the image, scan it directly if it is a QR code. If the orientation is wrong, try rotating, flipping, or enlarging it.

10. A Complete Solving Example

Suppose the challenge gives you data.csv:

id,pos,value,valid
1,3,g,1
2,0,f,1
3,9,x,0
4,1,l,1
5,2,a,1

The challenge gives no extra hints and only says “find the flag”.

First, observe the fields:

  • pos: looks like an order.
  • value: looks like a character.
  • valid: looks like a filtering condition.

So the idea is:

  1. Read the CSV.
  2. Keep only rows where valid=1.
  3. Sort by pos from small to large.
  4. Concatenate value.

Final script:

import csv

filename = "data.csv"
items = []

with open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8", newline="") as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)

    for row in reader:
        if row["valid"] != "1":
            continue

        pos = int(row["pos"])
        value = row["value"]
        items.append((pos, value))

items.sort(key=lambda item: item[0])

flag = "".join(value for pos, value in items)
print(flag)

Run it:

python solve.py

Output:

flag

In a real contest, the output is usually:

flag{xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx}

11. General Workflow for Solving CSV Challenges

When you encounter a CSV challenge, follow this process:

  1. Check the file name and description: look for hints such as sorting, coordinates, colors, encoding, logs, or traffic.
  2. Check the header: field names usually point to the intended solving direction.
  3. Print the first few rows: confirm what each column looks like.
  4. Count rows and inspect field ranges: judge whether it may be image coordinates, ASCII, or a time series.
  5. Write the smallest script first: make it read, print, and filter.
  6. Add features step by step: sort, concatenate, decode, and draw.
  7. Search for the flag format: finally use regex to extract flag{...}.

12. Common Pitfalls

12.1 Encoding Errors

If utf-8 fails, try:

open(filename, "r", encoding="gbk", newline="")

Or:

open(filename, "r", encoding="utf-8-sig", newline="")

utf-8-sig can handle CSV files with a BOM.

12.2 Numeric Sorting Turns into String Sorting

Wrong example:

items.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])

If x[0] is a string, the order may become:

1, 10, 11, 2, 3

So convert it to an integer:

index = int(row["index"])

12.3 The Separator Is Not a Comma

Some files are called CSV, but actually use semicolons, tabs, or vertical bars as separators.

reader = csv.DictReader(f, delimiter=";")

Tab-separated:

reader = csv.DictReader(f, delimiter="\t")

12.4 The Header Contains Spaces

Some headers may look like this:

 index , char

This will make row["index"] fail. Print the field names first:

print(reader.fieldnames)

Or strip keys after reading:

row = {k.strip(): v.strip() for k, v in row.items()}

Published on 2026-05-15 at M15tak3のBlog, last modified on 2026-05-15

All articles on this blog are licensed under the BY-NC-SA license agreement unless otherwise stated. Please indicate the source when reprinting!

item.title: When AI Starts Bringing Us Closer to Ithaca...
item.author: skyman.soul@gmail.com (M15tak3)
item.description:
When AI Starts Bringing Us Closer to Ithaca...

Author: M15tak3(skyman.soul@gmail.com)

When AI Starts Bringing Us Closer to Ithaca…

At some point, I realized my first reaction to a problem had become: ask AI.

At first, that felt exciting. AI was like a lamp you can switch on at any moment, lighting up corners that once took a long time to explore alone. If I did not understand code, I asked it. If I could not explain an error, I asked it. If I had no idea how to start a CTF challenge, I asked it. Web, Reverse, Pwn, Crypto: things that used to require endless searching and trial-and-error suddenly felt much closer.

This is real progress, and I do not want to deny that. AI lowers barriers and removes a lot of meaningless friction. People who were blocked by environment setup, unfamiliar terms, and fragmented documentation can now enter the field more smoothly. For security learners, that help is real. It gives difficult knowledge a softer entry point, and in lonely study hours, it can feel like a tireless companion.

But at the same time, something else is quietly becoming lighter.

In the past, getting stuck in CTF was normal. You could stare at a code fragment and see nothing. You could build payload after payload and get silence. You could get lost in decompiled output, search through traffic captures for one anomaly, and guess the next move of a program from registers and stack frames. Learning was slow. Many nights ended with no beautiful result, only a pile of failed attempts.

Yet when I look back, what stayed with me was exactly that slowness. The moments without immediate answers taught me how to observe. Wrong guesses taught me where the boundaries were. Problems I could not solve for a long time taught me that security is not remembering vulnerability names, but holding on to doubt inside uncertainty.

Now answers arrive too quickly. Sometimes before we have even sat with the question, we rush to submit something. A fluent explanation can replace half an hour of silent reasoning. A complete-looking solution path can make us believe we already understand.

That makes me a little sad. Not because AI is too powerful, but because we are becoming less able to tolerate the state of “not knowing.” Not knowing used to be the most natural entry to learning. It forced us to pause, to admit blank space, to move step by step toward the unknown. Now that blank space gets filled almost instantly. A confident answer appears on screen, well-structured, patient, polished. Anxiety fades for a while.

But understanding may fade with it.

In CTF, the most important thing was never only the flag. The flag can be an endpoint, or just a signal. What truly changes us is the path we walk toward it. Why did you suspect this point? Why does this route work? Why did the other direction fail? Why does the same payload break in a different environment? If we skip that journey and only see the final string, what remains inside is often empty.

AI is excellent at giving answers, but not always at helping people own answers. In cybersecurity, this is especially risky. Security is judgment. It is sensitivity to boundaries. It is distrust of things that “look normal.” AI can explain vulnerabilities, generate scripts, and speak with technical fluency. But it can also be wrong, and often wrong in ways that look right. Smoothness can make errors look respectable, and guesses look like conclusions. Without verification ability, it is easy to mistake that polish for truth.

Sometimes AI feels like a faster road that helps us avoid muddy paths. But some of our strength is built in that mud. We avoid detours, but we also lose time wrestling with real questions. We reach results faster, but not always the essence.

This is not an anti-AI argument. I will keep using it, and I know I depend on it. It helps me see blind spots, save time, and find a starting point in chaos. I just hope I do not forget this: tools can light the road, but they cannot grow eyes for me.

In CTF, maybe the best way to use AI is not to let it replace problem-solving, but to let it accompany us while we stay in front of the problem. It can suggest a direction, but we still need to decide whether there is really a path. It can help write a script, but we still need to know why every line exists. It can produce an answer, but we still need to ask: is this an answer I truly understand?

We are entering an age where answers are cheaper than ever. Precisely because of that, slowing down becomes more precious. Slow is not backward, and it is not incompetence. Slow is respect for the problem. Slow is a way to keep understanding for ourselves.

When everything is compressed into progress bars, outcomes, screenshots, and summaries, slowing down takes courage.

In cybersecurity, rushing is dangerous. Security is not a speed quiz; it is verification. Saying a vulnerability name first does not mean being closer to truth. Producing a payload first does not mean truly understanding the system. Security needs patience, skepticism, and the willingness to pause where things seem reasonable. Many vulnerabilities are not discovered by speed, but by repeated observation, repeated verification, and repeated questioning.

So I hope we can still learn to slow down.

Not to reject AI. Not to return to the past. But to keep a healthy distance while using it. Look at the challenge first. Think about boundaries first. Let the question stay in your head for a while.

Do not rush to cover all blank space with answers, and do not rush to use generated content to silence your insecurity.

Some blank space is worth keeping.

Because that is where thinking begins.

AI can bring us to Ithaca faster.

But before arrival, I still want to know whether we truly saw the sea.

Published on 2026-05-07 at M15tak3のBlog, last modified on 2026-05-07
